coredata.dc:title: Baicalin functionalized PEI-heparin carbon dots as cancer theranostic agent
coredata.dc:creator: Meher, Mukesh Kumar
Unnikrishnan, B.S.
Tripathi, Deepak Kumar
Packirisamy, Gopinath
Poluri, Krishna Mohan
coredata.dc:description: The worldwide prevalence of cancer and its significantly rising risks with age have garnered the attention of nanotechnology for prompt detection and effective therapy with minimal or no adverse effects. In the current study, heparin (HP) polymer derived heteroatom (N, S-) co-doped CDs were synthesized using hydrothermal synthesis method to efficiently deliver natural anticancer compound baicalin (BA). Heparin carbon dots (HCDs) were passivated with polyethylenimine (PEI) to improve its fluorescence quantum yield. The surface passivation of CDs by polycationic PEI polymer not only facilitated loading of BA, but also played a crucial role in the pH-responsive drug delivery. The sustained release of BA (up to 80 %) in mildly acidic pH (5.5 and 6.5) conditions endorsed its drug delivery potential for cancer-specific microenvironments. BA-loaded PHCDs exhibited enhanced anticancer activity as compared to BA/PHCDs indicating the effectiveness of the nanoformulation, Furthermore, the flow cytometry analysis confirmed that BA-PHCDs treated cells were arrested in the G2/M phase of cell cycle and had a higher potential for apoptosis. Bioimaging study demonstrated the excellent cell penetration efficiency of PHCDs with complete cytoplasmic localization. All this evidence comprehensively demonstrates the potency of BA-loaded PHCDs as a nanotheranostic agent for cancer.

(A) Schematic showing the green synthesis procedure of HCDs and PHCDs using hydrothermal method. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of HCDs and PHCDs. Photoluminescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths (with increment of 10 nm), and their respective normalized FL spectra of (C, D) HCDs and (E, F) PHCDs. (G) Photostability profiles of HCDs (λex: 370 nm and λem: 440 nm) and PHCDs (λex: 450 nm and λem: 520 nm).

(A) Graph depicting the comparative quantum yields of CDs and BA-loaded CDs (BA2: 200 μM of BA; BA4: 400 μM of BA). Fluorescence quenching profile of (B) HCDs and (C) PHCDs upon the addition of BA (0–400 μM).

TEM micrographs and diameter frequency histograms of (A, E) HCDs, (B, F) BA-loaded HCDs, (C, G) PHCDs and (D, H) BA-loaded PHCDs. XPS spectra of (I) HCDs and (J) PHCDs with percentage elemental analysis. (K) Comparative FTIR spectra of HP (black), HCDs (red), PEI (green), PHCDs (blue), BA (pink), HCDs+BA (aqua), and PHCDs+BA (dark blue).
(A) Schematic for BA loading to CDs in water and PBS conditions. (B) In vitro pH-dependent BA release profile from BA-loaded PHCDs.

Graphs showing the effect on cell viability of (A, C) NIH/3T3 cells and (B, D) A549 cells upon the co-exposure of CDs and BA-loaded CDs at different concentrations. * denotes the significant; if p ≤ 0.05 (*), p ≤ 0.01 (**), and p ≤ 0.001 (***).

Fluorescence microscopic images of AO/EB stained A549 cells treated with CDs, BA-loaded CDs, and BA. From left, Column 1: bright field, Column 2: GFP filter, Column 3: RFP filter, Column 4: merged GFP + RFP, Column 5: merged BF + GFP + RFP. Scale bar: 200 μM.

(A) Flow cytometry plots for cell cycle analysis. Row 1: Dot plot of FSC vs. SSC; Row 2: Cell cycle analysis of treated sample according to the DNA content from the gated region. (B) Quantification of flow cytometry data for the different phases of cell cycle. (C) BAX/Bcl-2 ratio in CDs and BA-loaded CDs treated A549 cells. * denotes the significant, if p ≤ 0.05 (*), p ≤ 0.01 (**), and p ≤ 0.001 (***).

(A) Fluorescence microscopic images of A549 cells showing the uptake of CDs and BA-loaded CDs for bioimaging application. From left, Column 1: bright field, Column 2: GFP filter, Column 3: merged images of BF with GFP. Scale bar: 200 μM. (B) Overlay of fluorescence graphs obtained from all samples in the flow cytometry. (C) The ratio of FL intensity of CDs and protein concentrations from lysed A549 cells after 24 h treatment.

Schematic showing the complete process of BA-loaded PHCDs by nanotheranostic approach intended for cancer. (A) carbonization of HP polymer, (B) PEI surface passivation, (C) efficient loading of BA to PHCDs, (D) cellular uptake of BA-loaded PHCDs by plasma membrane penetration, (E) BA release in the cancer cell cytoplasm, (F) interaction of CDs with genetic material leading to cell cycle arrest, (G) BA promoting cellular apoptosis in cancer cell.

head.ce:title: Baicalin functionalized PEI-heparin carbon dots as cancer theranostic agent

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, Roorkee 247667, Uttarakhand, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, Roorkee 247667, Uttarakhand, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee (IIT-Roorkee), Roorkee 247667, Uttarakhand, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The worldwide prevalence of cancer and its significantly rising risks with age have garnered the attention of nanotechnology for prompt detection and effective therapy with minimal or no adverse effects. In the current study, heparin (HP) polymer derived heteroatom (N, S-) co-doped CDs were synthesized using hydrothermal synthesis method to efficiently deliver natural anticancer compound baicalin (BA). Heparin carbon dots (HCDs) were passivated with polyethylenimine (PEI) to improve its fluorescence quantum yield. The surface passivation of CDs by polycationic PEI polymer not only facilitated loading of BA, but also played a crucial role in the pH-responsive drug delivery. The sustained release of BA (up to 80 %) in mildly acidic pH (5.5 and 6.5) conditions endorsed its drug delivery potential for cancer-specific microenvironments. BA-loaded PHCDs exhibited enhanced anticancer activity as compared to BA/PHCDs indicating the effectiveness of the nanoformulation, Furthermore, the flow cytometry analysis confirmed that BA-PHCDs treated cells were arrested in the G2/M phase of cell cycle and had a higher potential for apoptosis. Bioimaging study demonstrated the excellent cell penetration efficiency of PHCDs with complete cytoplasmic localization. All this evidence comprehensively demonstrates the potency of BA-loaded PHCDs as a nanotheranostic agent for cancer.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer has emerged as the leading cause of the rising global health burden due to its high incidence and mortality. According to WHO, it has been estimated that nearly 10 million people died from cancer in 2020, which accounts for one in six deaths. Moreover, 27.5 million new cancer cases and 16.2 million cancer-related fatalities are estimated every year by 2040 [1]. Despite remarkable progress in cancer drug discovery, the patient survival rate after treatment is extremely low. Most preferred diagnosis methods are generally imaging techniques such as computed tomography (CT), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), positron emission tomography (PET), and ultrasonography, which also have potential drawbacks of being costly, unavailable, less sensitive, and authentic without biopsy [2]. Further, different types of cancer require specific therapeutic procedures like radiotherapy and chemotherapy followed by surgical removal of tumor tissue. The existing chemotherapeutic drugs face significant challenges in distinguishing cancer cells from normal cells, which often leads to adverse effect upon administration [3,4]. For an effective cancer therapy, early detection combined with specific anti-cancer therapies are imperative. In this context, nanotheranostics have been formulated for the treatment of cancer by combining the diagnostics and therapeutics into single platform by fabricating novel nanomaterials [5,6].

Among various nanomaterials, tunable fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as one of the cutting-edge/ revolutionary green nanomaterial due to its enticing opto-electrochemical characteristics and their broad applications in bioimaging and nanomedicine [7,8]. CDs are nano‑carbons, also designated as carbon quantum dots, and have similar functionalities to semiconductor carbon dots. In recent years, CDs are mostly favored over semiconductor carbon dots because of their negligible toxicity, cost-effectiveness, green, and facile synthesis [9,10]. CDs are predominantly composed of sp2/sp3 carbon networks in a size range of 2–15 nm with various functional group(s) [11]. The CDs often possess the functional group(s) that are majorly present in the native composition of carbon-rich source materials like citric acid, lactic acid, glycerol, urea, glucose, amino acids, and polysaccharides etc. [12–18]. It is worth noting that, most quantum-sized CDs also confer a strong surface tension in the solution, which often leads to aggregation and bigger particle formation during the carbonization process. Hence, to improve the stability and quantum yield, several surface passivating agents are functionalized or conjugated to modify the surface of CDs [19]. The passivation can be done during the synthesis process, where the passivating agents were supplemented with precursor molecules or chemically attached in post-synthesis modification, which evidently improves the intrinsic properties of CDs [20]. The passivating agents also render active functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl, which are favorable for loading cargo/therapeutic drug molecules [21–23]. Polyethyleneimine (PEI), is a hydrophilic cationic polyelectrolyte extensively used as surface passivating agent for both CDs and quantum dots. Branched PEI contains all three forms of amino groups i.e., primary (−NH2), secondary (-NH-) and tertiary (-N=), enabling the conjugation of negatively charged drug molecules or biomolecules (Fig. 1A) [24–27]. Hailing et al. have fabricated PEI passivated CDs by the one-step microwave hydrothermal carbonization method and subsequently loaded with a model anticancer drug i.e. Doxorubicin (DOX) for tumor targeted drug delivery [28]. Compared to other nanocarriers for drug delivery, carbon dots are one of the most favorable nanomaterials due to its intrinsic properties like biocompatibility, non-toxic behavior, photostability, hydrophilic nature and ease of surface modifications. Carbon dots have also been used in live cell imaging which endows its dual functionality in cancer treatment in terms of diagnostic applications and targeted drug delivery [29].

In this context, the current study is designed to synthesize biocompatible carbon dots using the natural polysaccharide heparin (HP) for delivering the plant based anticancer agent Baicalin (BA) (Fig. 1B and C). Heparin (HP) is the most negatively charged biomolecule composed of repeating sulfated disaccharide units of idouronic acid and N-acetyl glucosamine. Due to this unique composition, the HP polymer chain essentially contains multiple functional groups majorly sulfate (SO3 −), carboxyl (-COO−), hydroxyl (-OH), and a fair amount of amine (NH2) groups [30,31]. Sun et al. reported a facile hydrothermal synthesis of HP-derived N, S-doped CDs for bioimaging and Fe3+ ion detection [32]. Reportedly, the nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) doping on CDs exhibited enhanced fluorescence efficiency compared to the N- or S-doped CDs. The CDs produced from HP exhibited low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility with high quantum yield, substantiating their potential biomedical applications such as bioimaging, drug delivery, and monitoring.

Baicalin (BA) is extracted and isolated from the Chinese herbal plant Scutellaria baicalensis and inclusively used for its anti-inflammatory, anti-proliferative, and antitumor activities [33]. Multiple studies have reported BA's antitumor activity, and identified the mode of action to be through cell cycle arrest and promoting apoptosis in cancer cells. Further, it has been established that BA treatment abrogated the AKT pathway, reduced Bax/Bcl-2 ratio, cyclin D1, CDK4 protein level, and downregulated the oncomiRNAs [34–36]. Although BA has excellent potential for anticancer therapy, the low solubility of BA due to its hydrophobic nature is a significant challenge for delivering BA to the tumor cells. Lv et al. have recently shown that BA loading on folic acid conjugated-polyamidoamine dendrimers (FA-PAMAM) has improved water solubility, antitumor efficacy, and drug delivery of BA [37].

Considering the various advantages of these natural molecules in formulating the anti-cancer agents, in the present work, BA conjugated, and PEI passivated heparin carbon dots have been synthesized for tumor-specific delivery and monitoring. The N, S-doped HP‑carbon dots (HCDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization method with PEI as a surface passivation agent. The resulting PEI-functionalized HCDs (PHCDs) were comprehensively characterized with different physicochemical techniques and were found to be fluorescent with comparable quantum yields. pH-dependent BA release from BA-PHCDs complexes revealed that sustained release of BA was obtained ∼80 % at acidic pH 5.5–6.0 in 72 h, thus substantiating its suitability for cancer cell-specific drugs. Further, BA-loaded PHCDs were evaluated using cellular experiments evidenced for cellular uptake and cytotoxic activity of BA-PHCDs. In conjunction to the above inferences, the cell cycle, and the apoptosis analysis established the potency of BA-loaded PHCDs as potential nanotheranostic agent for cancer therapy.

Heparin sodium salt (HP, from Bovine intestinal mucosa), dimethyl sulphoxide (DMSO), fluorescein sodium salt, acridine orange (AO), and ethidium bromide (EB) were obtained from SRL, India. PEI (Mw ∼25 kDa, branched), baicalin, ribonuclease A, quinine sulfate, and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), were purchased from Sigma, India. Syringe filters with a 0.2 μm pore size, dialysis membrane tubing (Mw cutoff 2 kDa and 25 kDa) were purchased from VWR Lab Products Pvt. Ltd., India. Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM), trypsin-EDTA, penicillin-streptomycin, fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Himedia. For all the experiments, analytical grade chemicals were used, and all the solutions were prepared in Milli-Q water unless specifically mentioned.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PEI passivated heparin CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The PEI passivated heparin CDs (PHCDs) were prepared via hydrothermal carbonization process by following previously reported protocol with slight modifications [32]. HP and PEI (1:1) were added to 50 mL of Milli-Q water to obtain the final concentration of 1 mg/mL of both polymers. The solution mixture was stirred for 1 h at 25 °C for the complete dissolution of both the polymers. HP-PEI mixture solution was transferred to the PEEK (polyether ether ketone) vessel, and fitted tightly into the stainless-steel hydrothermal synthesis reactor. The HP-PEI solution containing reactor was kept in a muffle furnace at 200 °C for 5 h. The reactor assembly was cooled at room temperature, and the dark yellow/brown-colored solution was collected from the PEEK vessel. To obtain pure PHCDs, the yellow-colored solution was centrifuged at 11000 RPM for 45 min, and supernatant was filtered with 0.2 μM syringe filter. As HP contains both amine and sulfate groups in its polymer chain, N, S-doped heparin CDs (HCDs) were prepared with similar procedure from HP without adding PEI to the primary solution during the carbonization process. Both samples were concentrated at 25 °C by rotatory evaporator and lyophilized at −70 °C to obtain powdered samples for further studies.

The lyophilized powder samples of both CDs (PHCDs and HCDs) were dissolved in Milli-Q water to a final concentration of 100 μg/mL. Both the CDs solutions were stirred and sonicated for an hour to obtain a uniform solution. Under stirring conditions at 500 RPM, BA (20 mM, DMSO) solution was added dropwise to the CD solutions for a final BA concentration of 400 μM (BA4). The BA-CDs mixture solutions were kept for stirring over a period of 12 h at 25 °C. The unloaded BA was removed by dialyzing the BA-loaded CDs solutions using 2 kDa dialysis tubing for 12 h against Milli-Q water. After dialyzing twice, the BA-loaded CDs solutions were concentrated by evaporating extra water using a rotatory evaporator. Finally, BA-loaded CDs solutions were kept in the dark at 4 °C for characterization and biological studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and BA-loaded CDs

ce:section.ce:para: Freshly prepared CDs and BA-loaded CDs solutions were drop casted over carbon-coated copper TEM grids (Icon Analytical Equipment Pvt. Ltd.) of 200 mesh separately and kept in a laminar hood for air-dry. The CDs containing TEM grids were examined using a transmission electron microscope (FEI Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The TEM experiments were carried out thrice with an independent set of samples to obtain an accurate size estimation. The average size of CDs was calculated by measuring the size of ∼50 CDs using ImageJ software (Version 1.8.0, NIH, USA).

UV–Vis absorption spectra of freshly prepared HCDs and PHCDs were recorded in the wavelength range 250–500 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Eppendorf BioSpectrometer). The fluorescence spectra of CDs and BA-loaded CDs were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-4600, Japan). The emission maxima wavelength of CDs were determined by exciting HCDs from 350 to 450 nm, and PHCDs from 400 to 500 nm with a uniform increment of 10 nm. Photostability study was conducted for a time of 1200 s by exciting HCDs and PHCDs at their respective maximum fluorescence wavelength. BA induced fluorescence quenching measurements were recorded with a fixed concentration of HCDs and PHCDs at 100 μg/mL in water. 20 mM of BA in DMSO stock solution was used as titrant and added to CD solutions to a final concentration of 200 μM and 400 μM. Fluorescence spectra at each step of BA addition were recorded by exciting HCDs at 370 nm and PHCDs at 450 nm.

The quantum yield (QY) of HCDs and PHCDs were calculated separately by relative measurement method, where the integrated fluorescence intensities and UV–Vis absorbance were compared with reference fluorophore [38]. Quinine sulfate (QS, QY = 54 % in 0.1 M H2SO4) and fluorescein (FS, QY = 95 % in 0.1 M NaOH) were used as reference fluorophores for HCDs and PHCDs, respectively. The quantum yields were calculated by the following equation:
Φ S

where Φ is the quantum yield, I is the integrated fluorescence intensity, η is the refractive index of water (η = 1.33), and A is the absorbance intensity. The subscript S denotes the sample and R refers to the reference fluorophore. The quantum yields of BA-loaded CDs were also calculated using the same experimental procedure.
The surface zeta potential of freshly prepared CDs and BA-loaded CDs were studied with a ZETA sizer instrument (Nano ZS90 Nanoseries, Malvern, UK) in triplicates.

Freshly synthesized CDs and BA-loaded CDs samples were lyophilized, and 10 mg of each powder sample was mixed with 1 g of KBr powder to make pellets for FTIR spectroscopy. The sample mixed KBr pellets were characterized by using FTIR spectrometer (Thermo nicolet nexus) in the wavenumber ranging from 4000 to 450 cm−1 at a resolution of 1 cm−1. Lyophilized powder samples (10 mg) were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (PHI 5000 VersaProbe III) equipped with monochromatic Al Kα radiation. The obtained XPS spectra were analyzed using PHI MultiPak software.

The lyophilized powder samples of CDs and BA-loaded CDs (10 mg for each) were heated in a thermogravimetric analyzer (EXSTAR SII 6300) up to 600 °C at the rate of 10 °C/min. The net weight loss for each sample was calculated and compared to analyze the thermal stability of CDs and its subsequent conjugates.

BA loading efficiency was evaluated in two different solvents i.e. water and PBS buffer at pH 7.4. Lyophilized powder HCDs and PHCDs samples were dissolved separately in water and PBS buffer at 100 μg/mL. Subsequently, 400 μM of BA was added to CDs solutions under stirring conditions. After 12 h of stirring, all four BA-loaded CDs solutions were dialyzed two times for 6 h against water using 2 kDa dialysis tubing. Free BA concentration in the dialysate was measured by taking UV–Vis absorption at 274 nm and compared to the standard calibration curve of BA. The BA loading efficiency was calculated by the following equation:
Loading efficiency
%
=
Initial
BA
conc. − Free
BA
conc.

BA

× 100 %

After removing free BA from the solution, 2 mL of BA-loaded PHCDs solution was filled in dialysis tubing with a molecular weight cutoff of 2 kDa. Then the sample-filled dialysis tube was placed in 18 mL of PBS buffer of different pH i.e., 5.5, 6.0, 7.4, and 8.4 for dialysis. The concentration of free BA in the dialysate was monitored up to 72 h with a time interval of 6 h using UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Graphs were plotted between BA concentration in dialysate versus time to determine the release behavior of BA at different pH.

A549 human lung carcinoma cells and NIH/3T3 fibroblast cells were used for in vitro studies, including cellular uptake, bioimaging, cell viability, cell cycle analysis, and apoptosis assessment. Both cells were revived and cultured in DMEM medium (supplemented with 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin) at 37 °C in a CO2 incubator (CellXpert C170, Eppendorf) maintained with 5 % CO2 and 95 % humidity.

Cell viability of CDs and BA-loaded CDs was evaluated separately against A549 and NIH/3T3 cells following the previously reported protocol with minor modifications [39]. Approximately 104 cells were seeded in 96 well plates and incubated at 37 °C in a CO2 incubator for 24 h. After incubation, the cells were supplemented with CDs (HCDs and PHCDs) at five different concentrations i.e., 50 μg/mL, 100 μg/mL, 200 μg/mL, 400 μg/mL, and 800 μg/mL, and incubated for another 24 h. BA-loaded CDs (BA-HCDs and BA-PHCDs) were supplemented according to the concentrations of BA, such as 10 μM, 20 μM, 40 μM, 80 μM and 160 μM keeping the concentration of the CDs constant in each BA-loaded CDs at 100 μg/mL. Only BA in media was used as a control for this experiment. After incubation of 24 h, the media from each well were gently pipetted out and the cells were washed twice with PBS buffer. Then, each well was supplemented with 100 μL of MTT (1 mg/mL in PBS) solution, and incubated for 4 h in CO2 incubator at 37 °C. The MTT solution from each well was discarded; 100 μL of lysis buffer (DMSO with isopropanol) was added to each well and incubated for 15 min at room temperature. The optical density of each well was determined by taking UV–Vis absorbance at 570 nm using a plate reader (Epoch BioTek). For live-dead staining, treated and untreated cells were stained with AO/EB fluorescent dye. After the 24 h treatment, cells were washed with PBS buffer and incubated with 50 μL of AO/EB (1 mg/mL) staining solution for 10 min. Consequently, the stained cells were washed with PBS buffer to eliminate excess dye, and visualized under a fluorescence microscope (EVOS M5000, Thermo Fisher Scientific).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular uptake and localization studies
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular uptake efficiency of CDs and BA-loaded CDs in A549 cells was evaluated using 24 well plates in triplicate. 100 μg/mL of CDs and 160 μM of BA-loaded CDs were supplemented to DMEM media (∼2 mL) containing ∼105 cells in each well. The treated cells were incubated for 24 h in CO2 incubator and maintained with 5 % of CO2 and 95 % of humidity. After incubation, the media was removed, and the cells were washed with PBS three times to remove free or cell surface adhered CDs complexes. Cells were visualized under a fluorescence microscope (EVOS M5000, Thermo Fisher Scientific) with GFP (excitation range 450–490 nm) filter. Similarly, the second set of treated cells was washed with PBS, and trypsinized with 0.25 % trypsin-EDTA in DMEM media. Cells were centrifuged at 2000 RPM for 10 min, and cell pellets were washed with ice-cold PBS twice. The washed cells were resuspended in PBS buffer, and the geometric mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) of each cell was detected by flow cytometer (Attune™ NxT Flow Cytometer, Thermo Fisher Scientific) with blue laser filter (excitation at 488 nm). The third set was used to calculate and compare the fluorescence efficiency of CDs inside cells. The media was taken out from treated cells and washed with PBS twice. Then, the cells were lysed by adding 100 μL of lysis buffer (Tris with Tween 80) to each well and kept in CO2 incubator for 2 h. After lysis, protein concentrations in each well were quantified using the Bradford assay. The fluorescence intensities of each well-containing the samples/solutions were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-4600, Japan) by exciting the respective CDs sample(s) at their excitation maxima. The fluorescence intensity and protein quantity ratio were calculated for each CD sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell cycle analysis by flow cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: For cell cycle analysis, approximately 105 A549 cells were seeded in 6 well plates and incubated at 37 °C in a CO2 incubator for 24 h. After incubation, the cells were supplemented with CDs (HPCD and PEI-CD) at 100 μg/mL, 160 μM of BA and BA-loaded CDs in 2 mL of complete DMEM media. The treated cells were incubated in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C for another 24 h. The media from each well was discarded, trypsinized with 0.25 % of trypsin-EDTA and washed twice with ice-cold PBS. Then, the cells were fixed with 70 % ethanol and kept at 4 °C for 2 h. The fixed cells were centrifuged at 2000 RPM for 10 min in a refrigerated centrifuge. Cell pellets were washed twice with PBS buffer to remove the traces of ethanol. The washed cell pellets were resuspended in 500 μL PBS buffer containing 0.1 mg/mL of ribonuclease A enzyme, stained with 0.02 mg/mL of propidium iodide (PI) dye, and kept in the dark for 15 min. Finally, the cells were analyzed on a flow cytometer with a yellow laser filter (excitation at 561 nm).

BA-triggered apoptosis in cancer cells was evaluated by calculating the relative expression levels of BAX and Bcl-2 genes in BA-treated cells [40,41]. Approximately 105 A549 cells in 6 well plates were treated with CDs (100 μg/mL)/BA-loaded CDs (160 μM) and incubated at 37 °C in a CO2 incubator for 24 h. Cells without any treatment were taken as a control. The RNA from treated and untreated cells was isolated using TRIzol reagent® [42]. The cDNA was synthesized using an iScript cDNA synthesis Kit (Biorad, USA). qRT PCR studies were done by incubating the reaction mixture and primers (Table S1) in a thermal cycler as per the manufacturer's instructions. The PCR products were detected, and average threshold cycle (Ct) values for each sample and the control were calculated for expression analysis. ∆Ct values for both targeted genes were calculated by subtracting the Ct value of one endogenous reference gene (β-actin). Further, ∆∆Ct values for each sample was calculated by subtracting the ∆Ct value of the control.

The experimental data acquired from different studies were plotted and analyzed by using SigmaPlot software (Version 14.0, Systat Software, Inc.). The average mean and standard error were calculated from the data obtained of three replicates. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and Tukey test were applied to define significant differences between data groups. The mean values were considered statistically significant if p ≤ 0.05 (*), p ≤ 0.01 (**), and p ≤ 0.001 (***).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis of heparin carbon dots and PEI passivated heparin carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: HP-derived CDs (HCDs) were synthesized by the hydrothermal (HT) treatment method, a convenient and rapid carbonization technique (Fig. 2A) [43]. The chosen HTL method is a green synthesis approach for obtaining CDs owing to the fact that no hazardous solvents/chemicals have been used; as HP polymer alone in an aqueous solution has been treated at a high temperature (i.e. 200 °C) with constant pressure for 5 h to complete the carbonization process. The thermal degradation and carbonization of HP comprising sulfated D-glucosamine, D-glucuronic acid, and L-iduronic acid is presumed to produce heteroatom (N- and S-) doped CDs, as the dissociation of polymeric carbon atoms conjugated to amine and sulfate groups enables the formation of N-, S-doped CDs during carbonization process [32]. Apart from HCDs, PEI passivation of CDs (PHCDs) was also achieved using the same protocol. The branched PEI was added to the HP mixture prior to carbonization process to passivate the CDs.

In such a scenario, branched PEI polymer does not undergo carbonization process, and only assists in passivating the surface of CDs [19,44]. The formation of the CDs (HCDs/PHCDs) has been confirmed from the visible color change in the solution from transparent to pale yellow, and strong blue-green color luminescence under UV light (365 nm) exposure [45]. The observable emission intensity of PHCDs was found to be enhanced as compared to HCDs, manifesting to the improved optical properties after PEI passivation. The optical properties of HCDs and PHCDs were thoroughly studied using UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–Vis spectrum of HCDs showed a strong peak at 280 nm, which was attributed to π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 hybridized CC bonds [46,47]. Whereas, PHCDs showed an intense peak at 354 nm, and a shoulder peak at 280 nm that can be attributed to n → π* transitions from nitrogen lone pairs, and π -π* transition of carbon atoms respectively (Fig. 2B) [48]. As the CDs solutions were illuminated under UV light, these intrinsic photoluminescence properties of CDs was further evaluated by determining their maximum excitation wavelength [49].

Upon exciting HCDs in the range of 350–450 nm wavelength with a continuous increment of 10 nm, the emission spectra resulted in a red-shifted pattern in ascending order and found to be in descending order for fluorescence intensities (Fig. 2C). The higher fluorescence emission for HCDs were obtained at 440 nm while exciting at 370 nm. Similarly, the PHCDs were excited 400–500 nm with an interval of 10 nm, and it was found that the spectra were red shifted with the maximum emission activity at 520 nm upon the excitation at 450 nm (Fig. 2E). Normalized emission graphs (Fig. 2D and F) depicted the shifting of emission peaks for HCDs and PHCDs towards longer wavelengths by increasing excitation wavelengths. For maximum fluorescence emission, the excitation and emission wavelengths of: 370 nm (λex for HCDs), 450 nm (λex for PHCDs), 440 nm (λem for HCDs), and 520 nm (λem for PHCDs) were used for further experiments. For photostability assessment, both CDs were excited for 20 min at their respective highest emission wavelengths, resulting in constant emission without any significant reduction of emission intensities (Fig. 3G). Comparing the fluorescence properties of both CDs, PHCDs showed enhanced fluorescence activity in visible range, which can be exploited as an innocuous fluorescence probe for biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Baicalin loaded HCDs and PHCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of BA-loaded CDs was carried out by following the electrostatic interaction-based adsorption of BA on the CDs surface. Briefly, the BA in DMSO solution was added dropwise to the aqueous solution of HCDs/PHCDs (100 μg/mL), and the adsorption of BA to CDs was primarily corroborated by evaluating the intrinsic fluorescence QY of the CDs. As both CDs have different emission maxima wavelengths at 435 nm and 520 nm, two different reference fluorophore molecules such as quinine sulfate (for HCDs) and fluorescein (for PHCDs) were used to calculate the fluorescence QY [32,38]. The QY obtained was 2.9 ± 1.2 % for HCDs and 9.4 ± 1.6 % for the PHCDs, which indicates the three times increase in fluorescence quantum yield by the PEI passivation to CDs (Fig. 3A). Further, the BA-induced FL quenching was also confirmed by calculating the QY of CDs supplemented with BA at two different concentrations (200 μM and 400 μM). After BA loading, the obtained QYs of HCDs+BA were 2.4 ± 0.8 % at 200 μM and 1.6 ± 0.3 % at 400 μM of BA; whereas QYs of PHCDs+BA were 5.8 ± 0.9 % at 200 μM and 2.3 ± 0.4 % at 400 μM of BA (Fig. 3A).

The significant reduction in fluorescence QY after BA loading indicated the BA-induced fluorescence quenching phenomena. To validate the BA induced fluorescence quenching, two different concentrations of BA, 200 μM and 400 μM of BA were titrated against CDs separately (Fig. 3B and C). By gradually increasing the BA concentrations, the fluorescence intensities of HCDs and PHCDs were gradually decreased, and higher FL quenching was observed with PHCDs, thus suggesting the effective BA interaction with its CDs surface. Further, the stability of BA-loaded CDs and the interaction of BA with CDs were confirmed by using different characterization techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical characterization of heparin-based CDs and BA-loaded CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The size, stability and morphological characteristics of CDs and BA-loaded CDs were studied with various physicochemical characterization techniques. The average zeta potential of HP was −50.8 ± 8 mV, while after carbonization, the zeta potential of HCDs was reduced to −2.4 ± 2 mV indicating the retention of both positive (amine) and negative (sulfate) charges on the surface of CDs (Fig. S1). Likewise, the zeta potential for the physical mixture of PEI and HP solution was −18.9 ± 5 mV; and after PEI passivation to CDs, the zeta potential was found to be 26.8 ± 6.1 mV. The higher positive zeta potential (more than +20 mV) of PHCDs confirms the surface passivation of PEI on the CDs [50]. The abundance of the amine groups on PEI revamps the positive charge surface for loading negative charged molecule like BA having three hydroxyl groups at the position of C-5, C-6, and C-7, and bestows its effective loading to PHCDs based on electrostatic interactions [51]. In contrast, the zeta potential of BA-loaded HCDs (HCDs+BA) and BA-loaded PHCDs (PHCDs+BA) were − 0.1 ± 0.2 mV (precipitated) and 21.8 ± 2.4 mV respectively, suggesting that adding BA to these CDs compensated for some of the positive charges of CDs. In fact, the low stability HCDs in solution was immediately precipitated after the addition of BA.

Further, the surface charge-based stability and morphological changes were evaluated by TEM. The obtained TEM micrographs (Fig. 4A-D) and histogram analysis (Fig. 4E-H) revealed the average size of HCDs and PHCDs were 3–4 nm and 5–6 nm respectively; while the BA addition increased the average size of HCDs+BA and PHCDs+BA to ∼100 nm and ∼ 15 nm respectively. In case of BA-loaded HCDs, the TEM micrographs clearly show a bigger cluster formation of CDs upon adding BA, indicating its unstable nature with BA molecules. On the contrary, the size of PHCDs increased by 13 ± 2 nm upon adding BA, and observed to be more stable as the CDs were uniformly distributed without any visible micro-aggregates. These morphological changes and stability/aggregation of CDs might have impacted the inherent fluorescent activity of CDs as discussed in the above section [52,53].

The PEI passivation on HPCDs was confirmed by XPS-based elemental composition analysis. The comparative analysis of full scan spectra of HPCDs resulted in elements C, O, N, and S with 76.5 %, 17.1 %, 3.1 %, and 3.3 %, respectively. In the case of PHCDs, the obtained weight% of C, O, N, and S were 65 %, 12.6 %, 21.2 %, and 0.9 %, respectively (Fig. 4I and J). The significant increase of nitrogen content in PHCDs by seven times higher than HPCDs indicates the presence of PEI owing to their nitrogen in the form of amine groups [54].

Further, FTIR spectroscopy results established the PEI functionalization to CDs and subsequent BA loading (Fig. 4K). The broad peak ranges from 3200 to 3600 cm−1 are attributed to OH and NH bonds stretching vibrations, and these peaks are relatively consistent in each sample showing the presence of a hydroxyl group. An intense peak between 1680 and 1620 cm−1 is ascribed to CC bond stretching, which is also expected to be present in all samples. The appearance of a sharp peak near 1080 cm−1 in HCDs sample is attributed to the SO sulfate bond stretching, which is in line with the sulfate group stretching in FTIR spectrum of HP sample [55]. Comparing the FTIR spectra of PEI and PHCDs, two similar peaks at 1636 cm−1 and 1090 cm−1 attributed to the NH bending and CN stretching of amine (both primary and secondary) groups established the successful PEI-based surface passivation [56]. The FTIR characteristic peaks for BA were a broad peak ranges from 3200 to 3500 cm−1 attributed to OH stretching, an intense peak at 1636 cm−1 attribute to CO stretching, and several weak peaks between 900 cm−1 to 1350 cm−1 can be assigned to aromatic CH bending and CC bond skeletal vibrations [57–59]. The appearance of additional FTIR peaks at 950 cm−1 and 1003 cm−1 in both HCDs+BA (weak) and PHCDs+BA (intense) can be attributed to NO bond vibrations and aromatic CH bending. The appearance of additional peaks between 950 and 1400 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of PHCDs+BA established the presence of BA on the surface of CDs.

Furthermore, TGA analysis of CDs and BA loaded CDs were performed to confirm the PEI surface passivation and BA loading for CDs (Fig. S2). For HCDs, the thermal profile follows multi-steps weight degradation and up to ∼33 % of total weight loss observed at 600 °C which is due to the loss of water molecules and elemental dopants. Similar thermal degradation profile has also been observed for BA loaded HCDs. However, the PHCDs lost <85 % of total initial mass at 600 °C signifying the presence of PEI polymer with CDs. The BA conjugated PHCDs also have almost similar degradation profile, but the percentage of weight loss increase during 120–420 °C indicating the presence of BA to the PHCDs. A maximum 20 % of weight loss difference has been observed at ∼250 °C in the case of BA loaded PHCDs (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: BA loading efficiency and in vitro BA release at different pH
ce:section.ce:para: The BA loading efficiency experiment was carried out with PHCDs at two different solvents, i.e., water and PBS at pH 7.4 as explained in the experimental section (Fig. 5A). The % of BA loading was determined by calculating the free drug (BA) concentration after 12 h of double dialysis. The 2 kDa dialysis bag was used in the dialysis that allows the unbound BA (446.36 Da) to move out from the dialysis bag. The BA concentration in the dialysate was determined by comparing/analyzing it with the standard plot. The percentage of BA loading for two different solvents were calculated to be 94.4 ± 2.9 % in water, and 41.1 ± 3.4 % in PBS. Considering the high loading efficiency, BA-loaded to PHCDs in water conditions was used in further biological studies. After successfully loading BA to PHCDs, the release of BA was evaluated in PBS with four different pH (5.5, 6.0, 7.4, and 8.4) conditions in 72 h. A sustainable BA release profile was observed in pH 5.5 and 6.0, while an insignificant amount of BA release was detected in pH 7.4 and 8.4. The amount of BA release was calculated by comparing it with the standard plot and was found to be cumulatively up to 78 % and 82 % of BA in 72 h at PBS pH 5.5 and 6.0, respectively (Fig. 5B). The significant increase in BA release at pH 5.5 and 6.0 substantiates the pH-responsive BA delivery of PHCDs complex in a mild acidic pH environment. It is worth noting that such acidic pH (5.5 and 6.0) conditions mimic the tumor microenvironment, where pH ranges from 5.6 to 6.8 [60]. Thus, BA as a natural anticancer drug can be potentially delivered in pH regulated manner specifically to the tumor microenvironment by using PHCDs complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity evaluation of CDs and BA-loaded CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity study was carried out to evaluate the toxic effect of CDs and BA-loaded CDs in both cancer cells (A549) and normal cells (NIH/3T3). Initially, CDs alone (HCDs and PHCDs) were evaluated for their concentration-dependent compatibility characteristics. Five different concentrations such as 50, 100, 200, 400, and 800 μg/mL of CDs were studied in A549 and NIH/3T3 cells through the MTT assay. The obtained MTT results evidenced that both the CDs (HCDs/PHCDs) showed almost similar cell inhibition patterns in A549 and NIH/3T3 cells.

It was evident from this study that CDs are cytocompatible and did not exhibit toxic effect as >90 % of NIH/3T3 cells were viable even at a concentration of 400 μg/mL (Fig. 6A). In case of A549 cells, >90 % of viability was maintained at 100 μg/mL of CDs, and the cell viability of A549 cells gradually declined beyond 100 μg/mL concentration (Fig. 6A and B). At 200 μg/mL of CDs concentration, approximately 75 % of A549 cells were viable, which can be attributed to the enhanced intake of CDs due to increased plasma membrane fluidity of cancer cells causing cell death [61]. As NIH/3T3 cells persisted their growth >90 % at 100 μg/mL of CDs; this concentration of CDs was used for BA loading at different concentrations (10, 20, 40, 80, 160 μM), and the associated cell cytotoxicity was evaluated (Fig. 6C and D). The NIH/3T3 normal cell viability was retained up to 80 μM concentration in all three forms i.e. HCDs+BA, PHCDs+BA and BA, while at 160 μM of BA concentrations, the cell number was comparatively reduced. In A549 cells, the cytotoxicity profiles of all three BA forms were entirely different. BA with PHCDs showed maximum activity at 160 μM concentration by inhibiting almost 80 % of cell growth compared to control, while BA with HCDs complex showed up to 35 % of inhibition. BA alone showed 20 % cytotoxicity at 10 μM concentration, and the percentage of cell viability increased by raising the BA concentration, which can be attributed to the BA's low water solubility, and precipitating nature at higher concentrations (> 20 μM) [62].

Further, the cell viability of NIH/3T3 and A549 treated with CDs, and BA-loaded CDs was also evaluated by fluorescence microscopy-assisted AO/EB staining assay (Fig. 7 ). 100 μg/mL of CDs and 160 μM of BA in loaded form were used for the fluorescence microscopy assay. AO is a cell membrane permeable dye that can enter live and dead cells, while EB can only enter dead cells once cells lose their membrane integrity. The permeation of AO makes cells fluorescent green, and EB selectively enter into the necrotic or apoptotic cells, making them appear orange/red [63]. Hence, comparing the overlapped image of these two dyes can identify the population(s) of live/dead cells. The overlapped fluorescence microscopy images of NIH/3T3 cells did not show any significant amount of cell death. Most of the treated NIH/3T3 cells were compared with control cells and observed that both had similar intact structures with green fluorescent nuclei (Fig. S3). Further, A549 cells treated with HCDs, HCDs+BA, and BA were observed to be exclusively with green fluorescence; whereas the cells treated with PHCDs and PHCDs+BA were distinguishable due to the appearance of red fluorescence (Fig. 7). Comparing the results obtained, BA-loaded PHCDs had a higher percentage of non-viable cells (red fluorescence) due to the inherent cytotoxic effect of BA. The obtained fluorescence based live-dead staining results are in line with the results obtained from the cell viability study (Fig. 6). It is also worth noting that the BA-loaded PHCDs complex showed its cytotoxic effect only in A549 cells, which might be due to the sustained BA release in the low pH microenvironment of A549 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of BA-triggered cell cycle arrest and apoptosis in cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the inhibitory effect of BA-loaded CDs on the progression of the cell cycle, the treated A549 cells were stained with PI/Ribonuclease A, and cell cycle distribution was analyzed by flow cytometry. The percentage of cell cycle distribution for control in G0/G1, S, and G2/M phases were 73.7 ± 2.9 %, 13.1 ± 2.3 %, and 11.2 ± 1.2 % respectively. The exposure of CDs and BA-loaded CDs led to intensifying cell proportion ratio in the G2/M phase as 13.2 ± 2.3 % for HCDs, 14.5 ± 2.2 % for PHCDs, 15.4 ± 1.9 % for HCDs+ BA, 22.1 ± 1.9 % for PHCDs+BA, 12.9 ± 1.7 % for BA (Fig. 8A). Comparing the behavior of the treated cells with control undoubtedly evidences the significant increase of cell population in G2/M phase upon treatment with PHCDs+BA, thus suggesting for the inhibition of cellular proliferation (Fig. 8B). Further, to assess the BA induced apoptosis in treated A549 cells, the gene expression level of pro-apoptotic (BAX) and anti-apoptotic (Bcl-2) were quantified by qRT-PCR. The BAX/Bcl-2 ratios obtained in treated A549 cells are 0.24 ± 0.05 for HCDs, 0.48 ± 0.08 for PHCDs, 0.18 ± 0.03 for HCDs+BA, 3.04 ± 0.09 for PHCDs+BA, and 0.11 ± 0.05 for BA (Fig. 8C). The increased expression level of pro-apoptotic BAX gene in both BA-loaded CDs treated cells established the BA induced apoptosis. The modest changes of the pro-apoptotic BAX gene in only BA treated cells, which is closer to the HCDs+BA treated cells signify inefficient penetration into cells due to their instable nature/aggregation/precipitation in the aqueous solution as described in the previous section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging efficiency and localization of CDs and BA-loaded CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent fluorescence characteristics and biocompatibility features of HCDs/PHCDs can be exploited for cell imaging applications. The bioimaging studies of CDs and BA-loaded CDs carried out in A549 cells after 24 h incubation revealed that both CDs exhibited green fluorescence under the microscope (Fig. 9A). A comparison of the bioimaging efficiency suggested that PHCDs displayed enhanced cell permeability and better fluorescence activity than the HCDs. Furthermore, the PHCDs treated cells with a green fluorescent outer layer leaving the inner nuclear area dull depicted the localization of CDs majorly in the cytosolic region. BA-loaded HCDs showed comparatively less cell penetration efficiency and fluorescence activity due to their unstable structure and larger aggregate formation. The BA-loaded PHCDs exhibited intense and dispersed fluorescence features in contrast to their parent PHCDs counterpart. The scattering fluorescent behavior of BA-PHDCs can be attributed to the cytotoxic effect of BA, which can trigger cell death during incubation (24 h), thus allowing the CDs to ooze out of the cells.

To elucidate the quantitative nature of the fluorescent characteristics of all the CDs inside the cells, flow cytometry based comparative analysis was performed. The flow cytometry results showed that the highest fluorescence emission was obtained from the PHCDs treated cells, followed by BA-loaded PHCDs (Fig. 9B, Fig. S4), which is in line with the fluorescence quantum yield results (Fig. 3A). Although, the addition of BA has reduced the fluorescence activity of PHCDs up to 50 % and still showed comparatively higher fluorescence emission than the HCDs and HCDs+BA treated cells (Fig. 9B). These flow cytometry studies conclusively establish the bioimaging potential of PHCDs and their analogs. Further, to validate these results, the fluorescence intensities were correlated with the total amount of protein inside the cells.

In brief, the treated cells were incubated for 24 h, then lysed and the total protein content was quantitated. Furthermore, the obtained FL intensity of CDs and protein content of lysed cells after 24 h treatment have been evaluated to compare the post-treatment fluorescence efficiency of CDs (Fig. 9C). The comparative analysis showed that the FL intensity to protein ratio was significantly higher in the case of PHCDs and BA-loaded PHCDs compared to the HCDs nanosystem, thus supplementing the in-vitro bioimaging results.
Mechanistic insights into physicochemical, bioimaging and anticancer properties of Baicalin loaded heparin carbon dots.

HP and their derivatives have demonstrated great potential for the treatment of various cancer by actively interacting with the biomolecules facilitating cellular growth, proliferation, migration, immune system modulation and angiogenesis [64]. A recent study by Lee et al. reported that a nano-assembly of HP and DOX demonstrated significant cytotoxic efficiency to inhibit tumor growth and metastasis [65]. Considering the biofunctionalities of HP, N, S- doped CDs (HCDs) were produced from the carbonization of HP biopolymer, which comprises majorly disaccharide units functionalized with amine and sulfate groups. CDs synthesized from natural resources such as biomolecules and biopolymers via green synthesis method tends to have minimal cytotoxicity [7]. Sun et al. also reported that N, S- doped CDs from HP exhibits excellent fluorescence properties emitting blue-green fluorescence by UV-light exposure, water solubility, biocompatibility, and bioimaging efficiency [32]. For enhancement of optical and electrical properties of CDs, single or multi-atom [such as nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), phosphorous (P), and boron (B)] doped CDs were synthesized, which efficiently improved the fluorescence quantum yields, water solubility, molecular interactions and other physicochemical properties of CDs [66,67].

In HP derived CDs, the presence of both positively (N) and negatively (S) charged atoms on the surface of CDs significantly reduced the zeta potential (less than ±10 mV), and affected their stability in physiological conditions. To improve stability, a cationic polymer (PEI) having a high melting point (340–360 °C) was used as a surface passivating agent for CDs during the carbonization process [68]. As the carbonization of HP is carried out at 200 °C, PEI polymer has been attached to the surface of N, S-doped CDs to produce PHCDs. Under UV-light exposure, the PHCDs exhibited distinguishably more significant fluorescence emission than HCDs, suggesting that surface passivation improves the fluorescence properties of CDs [24,69]. In line with our results, Han et al. also reported the enhancement of fluorescence emission intensity for PEI passivated CDs, as compared to their bare counterparts [20].

It is worth noting that the higher surface zeta potential of CDs is quintessential to load a drug on its surface without any aggregation/stability effects. The PEI passivation improved the zeta potential from −2.4 ± 2 mV to 26.8 ± 6.1 mV. As the loading molecule BA contains hydroxyl groups and the interaction of BA molecules compensates for some positive charge on CDs, thus resulting in a stable PHCDs+BA complex with the zeta potential of 21.8 ± 2.4 mV. Indeed, the TEM results also evidenced the instability of HCDs+BA in terms of aggregates, while the size of PHCDs was uniformly dispersed and increased marginally to 10–15 nm after loading the anticancer agent BA (Fig. 4B). Indeed, the observed differential weight loss pattern of PHCDs and PHCDs+BA TGA profiles in the temperature range 120–420 °C confirming the loading of BA molecules onto PHCDs.

Interestingly, the PEI surface passivation has altered the optical properties of CDs by shifting the absorption and emission towards higher wavelengths, and also by adding a shielding layer to prevent adhesion and improve its fluorescence intensity [74]. The calculated quantum yield of PHCDs was enhanced by three times after PEI passivation. However, the BA addition to the PHCDs surface gradually quenched its fluorescence intensity. A study by Ding et al. reported that adding 25–500 μM of Fe3+ ions to N, S-CDs synthesized from α-lipoic acid gradually quenched its fluorescence intensity, ensuring their applicability in Fe3+ detection [75]. Comparative analysis of the current study with previously reported studies on N, S-doped/ PEI passivated CDs in terms of fluorescence quantum yield and drug loading efficiency for bioimaging / cancer drug delivery applications have been presented in Table 1 . Further, it is worth noting that the QY obtained from the PHCDs is at par with the QY of other CDs passivated with PEI. Interestingly, the abundance of surface amino groups in Cisplatin (IV) loaded CDs predominantly aid in the enhancement of fluorescence QY up to 25.5 %, which is comparatively two-fold of the QY obtained from PEI passivated CDs [20,27,28,72].

The drug loading study indicated that >90 % of BA can be loaded to PHCDs only in water. Interestingly, BA was significantly inefficient for loading in PBS (Fig. 5A), which might be due to the presence of sodium ions that hinders the BA loading onto PHCDs by their interaction with the negatively charged hydroxyl groups of BA [76]. Evidently, the PHCDs nanosystem has achieved maximum drug loading efficiency i.e. > 90 % which is higher than the previously reported PEI-CDs for drug delivery in cancer therapy (Table 1). However, the drug loading efficiency of PHCDs nanosystem majorly confined to the negatively charged drug compounds. The BA release study demonstrated acidic pH (5.5 and 6.0) sustain release up to 80 % of loaded BA from PHCDs suggesting pH specific drug delivery. At low pH, the amine groups present on the PHCDs gets protonated, thus breaking the hydrogen bonding interactions between the BA and PEI [28,77]. By adjusting the pH from 7.4 to 5.5, the release of BA molecule increased substantially by 8-fold, thus demonstrating the pH stimulating response of PHCDs. On similar note, Hailing et al. have also reported that the release of DOX drug from PEI-CDs increased by 10-fold in acidic pH 5.2 [28].

The cell viability of A549 cancer cells and NIH/3T3 normal cells with the treatment of HCDs and PHCDs revealed that cancer cells are more susceptible to CDs than normal cells NIH/3T3, which is in line with the study reported by Xie et al. [78]. Comparing the cellular toxicity of HCDs and PHCDs in A549 cells, both CDs followed a similar pattern of inhibition at lower concentrations while increasing the concentrations, PHCDs exhibited higher growth inhibition compared to HCDs might be due to the positively charged surface of PHCDs. According to the study by Fröhlich, positively charged nanoparticles have evidently shown higher plasma membrane disruption efficiency, cell penetration, mitochondrial damage, and autophagy compared to negatively charged nanoparticles [79]. Considering all PHCD's peculiarities, BA was loaded for better anticancer efficiency. As >90 % cell viability of NIH/3T3 and A549 cells were retained at 100 μg/mL of CDs concentration, thus, BA was loaded onto the same concentration of CDs assuming safe for cells. The aggregation nature of HCDs with BA and its instability might be the reason behind its inability to inhibit. PHCDs with 160 μM BA loading successfully inhibited >80 % of the A549 cell population compared to the control, which is evidently higher than the treatment with HCDs+BA and free BA at the same concentration. The BA and BA-loaded HCDs complexes even at the highest concentration also did not show significant inhibition in NIH/3T3 cells, as >80 % of them were viable. BA compound only showed around 20 % inhibition of cancer cell growth at lower concentration i.e. 10 μM and above that it precipitated. The aggregation of HCDs+BA complex and low bioavailability of BA ≥20 μM concentration for bigger particle formation could be the primary reason for their inefficient inhibition of cancer cells which is also evident in AO/EB stained fluorescence imaging (Fig. 6) [80].

Further, the anticipated inhibition mechanism for the growth of A549 cells was identified by analyzing the expression of pro−/anti-apoptotic genes and stages involved in cell cycle arrest. Gene expression studies suggested that, the BA-loaded PHCDs showed maximum upregulation of BAX gene as compared to others, thus confirming the apoptosis mediated cell death in HPCDs+BA treated cells [34,81]. Although only BA treatment exhibited anticancer activity, it is essential to deliver the BA molecules effectively into the cancer cells considering its hydrophobic nature, limited bioavailability, and solubility/stability. Indeed, majority of these physicochemical limitations have been successfully surmounted by loading the BA on to PHCDs [37,59]. The studies also suggested that the BA-loaded PHCDs complex has better cell penetration efficiency of PHCDs+BA endow efficient BA delivery, which as a result showed significant upregulation of BAX genes in cells. An extensive study by Tao et al. demonstrated that the dose dependent BA treatment induces apoptosis in HT-29 colon cancer cells in a gradually increasing manner. Further, the BA induced apoptosis was also confirmed by recovering the cellular viability of BA treated cells with the administration of an apoptotic inhibitor Z-VAD-FMK [34]. On similar lines, Sui et al. reported that BA treatment significantly upregulated the expression level of mitochondrial apoptotic proteins i.e. BAX and caspase-9; and also evidenced for the inhibition Akt/mTOR pathway by BA for cell cycle arrest, thus corroborating its underlying molecular mechanism for antitumor activity [35].

In addition to apoptosis, it has also been reported that only BA treatment for cancer cells induces G1/S phase cell cycle arrest. However, the current flow cytometry results demonstrated that the PHCDs+BA treatment resulted in elevated G2/M phase cell cycle arrest, which is comparatively more populated than G0/G1 and S phases (Fig. 7B). These results propound the possible intercalation of positively charged PHCDs (after BA release) with the negatively charged genetic materials (DNA/RNA) which probably might not have allowed the cells to enter the non-proliferative stage, and thus leading to cell death by apoptosis [82–84]. Considering all these above observations, the molecular mechanisms associated with the anticancer activity of BA-loaded PHCDs can be postulated by the following processes as described in Fig. 10 .

Apart from the anticancer activity, the fluorescent characteristics of CDs is a significant phenomenon for assessing their bioimaging ability. PHCDs in A549 cells showed excellent fluorescent activity, and are evenly distributed in the cytoplasm [85,86]. On similar lines, a recent study by Li et al. also demonstrated the concentration-dependent cellular uptake of PEI functionalized CDs in human glioblastoma cells, which can efficiently pass through the plasma membrane for image-guided siRNA delivery [87]. In addition to fluorescence microscopy, the flow cytometry results also confirmed that PEI-passivated CDs possess enhanced bioimaging efficiency. Thus, both the anticancer activity of BA-loaded PHCDs and the fluorescence/bioimaging properties of the PHCDs established in this study confirm the nanotheranostic properties of these heparin based carbon dots/nanocarriers.

In summary, highly fluorescent PEI-passivated CDs were synthesized from HP through a facile and green synthesis method. The surface passivation of PEI not only enhanced the fluorescence efficiency, but also facilitated the loading of BA. It is worth noting that >90 % of BA loading was achieved in aqueous conditions on PHCDs, which is barely soluble in water. Due to the presence of amine functional groups, the synthesized BA-loaded on PHCDs exhibited sustained BA delivery in mildly acidic pH (5.5 and 6.0) up to 80 % of loaded BA in 72 h duration. The study also demonstrated the cell penetrating efficiency and bioimaging capability of PHCDs and BA-loaded PHCDs in A549 cancer cells. The efficient BA delivery in lower pH and excellent fluorescent activity in cell imaging endows its potential for tumor-targeted BA delivery. Furthermore, the significant inhibition of cancer cell growth, G2/M phase cell cycle arrest, and BA-induced apoptosis established the BA-PHCD nanoformulation as a potential nanotheranostic agent for cancer.

Mukesh Kumar Meher: Conceptualization, Methodology, Experiments, Data Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft. B. S. Unnikrishnan: Methodology, Experiments, Data Formal analysis, Validation. Deepak Kumar Tripathi: Methodology, Experiments, Data Formal analysis, Validation. Gopinath Packirisamy: Resources, Writing – review and editing, Supervision. Krishna Mohan Poluri: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.
